schools.summary: Gill: this stands opposed to and is distinguished from the case and circumstances of Esau and his posterity in the preceding chapter, who dwelt in Mount Seir; Jacob dwelt in the land of his father's sojourning, the land of promise, as a stranger and pilgrim; the witnesses note Jacob dwelling in the land where his father was a stranger, the land of Canaan — the chosen line abiding as pilgrims in the promised land, set against Esau settled in Seir (the history returning to Jacob after the digression on Esau: 'Jacob dwelt in the land wherein his father was a stranger, in the land of Canaan'; the deliberate contrast, that, where Esau and his sons were now a settled and prosperous nation in their own Mount Seir, Jacob 'dwelt in the land wherein his father was a stranger,' abiding as a pilgrim and sojourner in Canaan, holding it not yet by possession but by promise; the dignity of this pilgrim estate, that Jacob, the heir of the covenant, kept to the land of promise though he held it only as his fathers had, strangers and pilgrims, waiting on God's word; the difference between the two brothers' portions, the one a present worldly kingdom, the other a promised inheritance held by faith, so Jacob dwells as a pilgrim in Canaan, the land of promise).

themes: And Jacob dwelt in the land wherein his father was a stranger
in the land of Canaan
Jacob the pilgrim in the land of promise, set against Esau in Seir
the covenant heir holding the land by promise and faith

schools.summary: Gill: that, no genealogy following, some interpret 'the generations of Jacob' of the events or things that befell Jacob and his family, particularly with respect to his son Joseph; the witnesses note Joseph, at seventeen, feeding the flock with his brethren and bringing their evil report to his father — the opening of the history of Jacob's family, centred on Joseph, and the first ground of his brethren's dislike (the record turning to 'the generations of Jacob,' which, no genealogy following, means the history of his house, and at once fixing on Joseph: 'Joseph, being seventeen years old, was feeding the flock with his brethren... and Joseph brought unto his father their evil report'; the young Joseph, 'seventeen years old,' tending the flock with his brothers, especially 'the sons of Bilhah, and... Zilpah,' the handmaids' sons; the matter of 'their evil report,' that Joseph brought to his father word of some wickedness among his brothers, the commentators differing whether this was a faithful and dutiful telling of real evil, Joseph's piety unwilling to hide their sin, or a tale-bearing that stirred their dislike, most taking it as a true report of real wrongdoing that Joseph in his integrity could not conceal; that this began to set the brothers against him, so the history of Jacob's house opens with Joseph and the evil report).

debates.question: What was Joseph's 'evil report' of his brethren?

sides.position: A true and dutiful report of real wrongdoing that Joseph, in his integrity and piety, could not conceal from his father
Some take it less favourably as tale-bearing that stirred the brothers' dislike

themes: Joseph, being seventeen years old, was feeding the flock with his brethren
and Joseph brought unto his father their evil report
the history of Jacob's house centred on Joseph
the true report of real wrongdoing that began the brothers' dislike

schools.summary: Gill: Israel loved Joseph more than all his children, he being the firstborn of his beloved Rachel, a lovely youth of beautiful aspect, very promising, prudent and pious, the reason given being that he was the son of his old age; Adam Clarke notes the coat of many colours (or long coat with sleeves) was a distinguishing mark of favour; the witnesses note Israel loving Joseph above all his children because he was the son of his old age, and making him a coat of many colours — the father's fond, favouring love and the token of it, the coat that marked Joseph out (the cause of the brothers' envy now told: 'Israel loved Joseph more than all his children, because he was the son of his old age: and he made him a coat of many colours'; the depth of Jacob's love for Joseph, the dear son of his beloved Rachel, born to him in his old age, lovely, wise, and godly, whom he 'loved... more than all his children'; the token of this love, 'a coat of many colours,' a long coat with sleeves, a garment of distinction such as marked a favoured son or one set apart, a visible mark of Jacob's special favour; the imprudence the commentators mark in this open favouritism, that Jacob, though loving Joseph for good reason, showed it too plainly and so sowed the seed of his brothers' envy and the troubles that followed).

themes: Israel loved Joseph more than all his children, because he was the son of his old age
and he made him a coat of many colours
the father's fond love and the coat that marked Joseph out
a love good in its ground, yet shown too plainly, sowing envy

schools.summary: Gill: they perceived their father's greater love by various things in his behaviour to him, by his words, his looks, his gestures, and particularly by the coat he had given him; the witnesses note the brethren, seeing their father loved Joseph more, hating him so they could not speak peaceably to him — the rise of the brothers' settled hatred, the bitter fruit of envy at their father's favour (the father's love bearing bitter fruit in the brothers: 'when his brethren saw that their father loved him more than all his brethren, they hated him, and could not speak peaceably unto him'; the cause and depth of the hatred, that the brothers 'saw,' by Jacob's words, looks, and the coat, that he 'loved him more than all,' and this stirred not grief but 'hatred,' a settled ill-will, so that 'they could not speak peaceably unto him,' could not give him a kind or civil word; the working of envy here, that the favour shown to one breeds hatred in others, and that the brothers, instead of striving to deserve their father's love, hated the brother who had it; in the typology the commentators draw, the foreshadowing of Christ, the beloved Son hated by his own, so the brothers' hatred of Joseph is settled and open).

debates.question: How do the commentators read Joseph's place in this chapter typologically?

sides.position: As a type of Christ — the beloved son of the father, hated and rejected by his own brethren, later stripped, sold, and exalted to save them

themes: when his brethren saw that their father loved him more than all his brethren, they hated him
and could not speak peaceably unto him
the brothers' settled hatred, the bitter fruit of envy
a foreshadowing of Christ, the beloved Son hated by his own

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph told the dream as a dream, in the simplicity of his heart, not understanding it or imagining there was any meaning in it, not with any design to affront them; the witnesses note Joseph dreaming a dream and telling it to his brethren, who hated him the more — the first prophetic dream, innocently told, kindling the brothers' hatred higher (God beginning to reveal Joseph's future in a dream: 'Joseph dreamed a dream, and he told it his brethren: and they hated him yet the more'; the nature of the dream, that it was no idle fancy but a God-sent dream, the first of two by which God foreshowed Joseph's coming exaltation over his family; the manner of Joseph's telling, 'he told it his brethren,' in the openness and simplicity of his heart, as the commentators note, not understanding its meaning nor seeking to provoke but innocently relating what he had dreamed; the effect, 'they hated him yet the more,' that the dream, far from being received, kindled their hatred higher, for they read in it, better than Joseph, a sign of his pre-eminence; that thus the very means God used to foreshow Joseph's glory became, through their envy, the occasion of their greater hatred, so the dream, innocently told, inflames the brothers' hatred).

themes: And Joseph dreamed a dream, and he told it his brethren
and they hated him yet the more
the first God-sent dream, told in simplicity of heart
the means of God's foreshowing made the occasion of greater hate

schools.summary: Gill: the Targums of Onkelos and Jonathan render it 'hear now,' immediately, directly, lest he should forget it, having perhaps dreamt it the night before; the witnesses note Joseph asking his brethren to hear the dream he had dreamed — the simple, open invitation to hear, the prelude to the telling of the first dream (Joseph, in his guilelessness, bidding them listen: 'Hear, I pray you, this dream which I have dreamed'; the openness of the request, that Joseph, 'I pray you,' courteously and without guile invites his brothers to 'hear... this dream,' eager as a young man to share the strange thing he had seen in sleep; the commentators' note on the urgency, 'hear now' as the Targums read, as if Joseph would tell it at once while it was fresh lest he forget; the innocence of Joseph in all this, that he suspected no harm and meant none, not seeing how his words would be taken; that this guileless invitation only set the stage for the brothers' fresh resentment when they heard the dream's import, so Joseph openly invites his brethren to hear his dream).

themes: And he said unto them, Hear, I pray you
this dream which I have dreamed
Joseph's guileless invitation to hear his dream
eager and without guile, suspecting no harm and meaning none

schools.summary: Gill: it was represented to his mind as if it were harvest time, and he and his brethren were at work together in the field binding up sheaves of corn; the witnesses note Joseph's dream of binding sheaves, his sheaf rising upright and the others bowing to it — the prophetic dream foreshowing Joseph's exaltation and his brothers' obeisance (Joseph relating the vision: 'we were binding sheaves in the field, and, lo, my sheaf arose, and also stood upright; and, behold, your sheaves stood round about, and made obeisance to my sheaf'; the imagery, a harvest scene, the brothers and Joseph 'binding sheaves,' and then the marvel, Joseph's 'sheaf arose, and... stood upright,' while 'your sheaves stood round about, and made obeisance to my sheaf,' bowing down to it; the plain meaning, which the brothers caught at once, that Joseph should be raised to honour and rule and his brothers should bow down before him; the fulfilment long after, when the brothers came to Egypt and bowed before Joseph the governor, buying corn, the harvest-sheaves fitly foreshowing the corn of Egypt; the providence of God in the dream, foreshowing what he would bring to pass through all the dark way between, so the sheaves bowing to Joseph's sheaf foreshow his exaltation).

themes: my sheaf arose, and also stood upright
your sheaves stood round about, and made obeisance to my sheaf
the first dream foreshowing Joseph's exaltation and his brothers' obeisance
fulfilled when they bowed before Joseph in Egypt, buying corn

schools.summary: John Calvin: here it is plainly shown that the brethren understood the dream and were stung by it; Gill: they were highly offended, understanding the dream and its meaning better than Joseph did; the witnesses note the brethren asking whether Joseph should indeed reign and have dominion over them, and hating him the more for his dreams and words — the brothers reading the dream's meaning and resenting the thought of Joseph's rule (the brothers catching at once the dream's import and spurning it: 'Shalt thou indeed reign over us? or shalt thou indeed have dominion over us? And they hated him yet the more for his dreams, and for his words'; the indignant double question, 'Shalt thou indeed reign... shalt thou indeed have dominion,' the brothers, understanding the dream as Calvin notes better than Joseph, reading in it a claim that he should be king and lord over them, and recoiling at the thought that their younger brother should rule them; that their hatred grew 'for his dreams, and for his words,' both for the dreams themselves and for the telling of them; the working of pride and envy, that they could not bear even the foreshadowing of Joseph's pre-eminence, so the brothers, reading the dream, hate Joseph the more).

themes: Shalt thou indeed reign over us? or shalt thou indeed have dominion over us?
And they hated him yet the more for his dreams, and for his words
the brothers reading the dream's claim of Joseph's rule
recoiling in pride and envy at the foreshadowing of his pre-eminence

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: this second dream was of the same import as the first, confirming it by different and more comprehensive emblems; Gill: it related to the same subject, the emblems being different and more comprehensive; the witnesses note Joseph dreaming a second dream of the sun, moon, and eleven stars making obeisance to him — the second dream, confirming the first by grander emblems, foreshowing the obeisance of his whole house (God confirming the foreshowing in a second dream: 'I have dreamed a dream more; and, behold, the sun and the moon and the eleven stars made obeisance to me'; the grander and more comprehensive emblems, that where the first dream showed the sheaves, the brothers, bowing, this shows 'the sun and the moon and the eleven stars' doing obeisance, the heavenly lights for the whole family, the sun for Jacob the father, the moon for the mother, and 'the eleven stars' for the eleven brothers; that God gave the dream twice, as later to Pharaoh, to confirm that the thing was established and would surely come to pass; that the dream foreshowed the bowing not of the brothers only but of the whole house of Jacob before Joseph, so the sun, moon, and stars bowing confirm and enlarge the foreshowing).

themes: I have dreamed a dream more
the sun and the moon and the eleven stars made obeisance to me
the second dream confirming the first by grander emblems
the whole house of Jacob bowing, the dream doubled to confirm it

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph told it to his father a second time in their hearing, that he might pass his judgment on it; Jacob rebuked him, perhaps to allay the envy of his brethren, though he observed the saying; the witnesses note Joseph telling the second dream to his father, and Jacob rebuking him, asking if all the family should bow to him — the father's rebuke of the dream, given to check the brothers' envy (Joseph telling the dream to Jacob as well, and the father reproving: 'his father rebuked him, and said unto him... Shall I and thy mother and thy brethren indeed come to bow down ourselves to thee to the earth?'; the substance of the rebuke, that Jacob, hearing the dream of the sun, moon, and stars, read its meaning at once, 'I and thy mother and thy brethren,' and 'rebuked' Joseph for it, as if it were a presumptuous fancy that the whole family should bow to him; the prudence the commentators mark, that Jacob rebuked Joseph partly to check his pride but chiefly to allay the brothers' envy, lest they think the father favoured the dream; that the rebuke was on the surface, for Jacob 'observed the saying' and kept it in his heart, pondering whether God might indeed mean it, so Jacob outwardly rebukes the dream to quiet the brothers).

themes: his father rebuked him, and said unto him, What is this dream that thou hast dreamed?
Shall I and thy mother and thy brethren indeed come to bow down ourselves to thee?
Jacob's rebuke to check pride and allay the brothers' envy
while inwardly he pondered whether God might mean it

schools.summary: Gill: the brethren envied him, suspecting and fearing the dreams portended Joseph's pre-eminence over them; but Jacob observed and kept the saying, waiting to see the issue; the witnesses note the brethren envying Joseph, while his father observed and kept the saying — the contrast of the brothers' envy and the father's thoughtful pondering (the dream working two ways: 'his brethren envied him; but his father observed the saying'; the two responses set side by side, the brothers 'envied him,' their hatred now ripened into settled envy, fearing the dreams foretold his rule over them, but Jacob 'observed the saying,' kept and pondered it in his heart, as Mary later kept the sayings concerning Jesus, waiting in faith to see what God would bring to pass; the wisdom of Jacob in this, that he neither lightly dismissed the dreams nor openly favoured them but laid them up and waited; the difference between the envious heart, which hates what it fears, and the believing heart, which waits on God, so the brothers envy while the father keeps the saying).

themes: his brethren envied him
but his father observed the saying
the contrast of the brothers' envy and the father's faith
Jacob pondering the saying, waiting on what God would bring to pass

schools.summary: Gill: this was very probably some time after the telling of the dreams; it was usual to remove flocks from place to place for pasture; the witnesses note the brethren going to feed their father's flock in Shechem — the brothers' departure to the distant pastures, the occasion of Joseph's fateful errand (the narrative moving toward the great deed: 'his brethren went to feed their father's flock in Shechem'; the time and place, that 'some time after' the dreams the brothers drove the flock to 'Shechem,' a good distance from Hebron where Jacob dwelt, for the sake of pasture as was the shepherds' custom to move the flocks where grass was; the irony the commentators mark in the place, that Shechem was the very city where the brothers had made the bloody slaughter, a place of evil memory, yet there they fed the flock; that their being far off at Shechem set the stage for Jacob's sending Joseph to see how they fared, the errand that would lead to his betrayal, so the brothers go to Shechem and the way to Joseph's trial is opened).

themes: And his brethren went to feed their father's flock in Shechem
the brothers' departure to the distant pastures
Shechem, the city of their bloody slaughter, far from Hebron
their being far off setting the stage for Joseph's fateful errand

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Israel said unto Joseph, Do not thy brethren feed the flock in Shechem? come, I will send thee unto them; the witnesses note Jacob asking Joseph whether his brethren fed the flock in Shechem and sending him to them, Joseph answering 'Here am I' — the father's errand and the son's willing obedience, all unknowing of the danger (Jacob, concerned for his sons and flock, sending Joseph: 'Do not thy brethren feed the flock in Shechem? come, and I will send thee unto them. And he said to him, Here am I'; the father's purpose, that Jacob would have word of his sons away at Shechem and of the flock, and so resolves to 'send' Joseph to them; the readiness of Joseph's answer, 'Here am I,' the prompt, dutiful obedience of a good son willing to go at his father's word, with no thought of the brothers' hatred or the peril before him; the typology the commentators draw, that here Joseph, like Christ the beloved Son, is sent by the father to his brethren who hated him, and goes willingly; the providence of God working through this simple errand toward the fulfilment of the dreams, so Jacob sends Joseph and Joseph answers, Here am I).

themes: Do not thy brethren feed the flock in Shechem? come, and I will send thee unto them
And he said to him, Here am I
the father's errand and the son's ready obedience
like Christ the beloved Son sent to his hating brethren, going willingly

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: Jacob bade him go see whether it be well with his brethren and well with the flocks, and bring word again; Gill: Jacob sent him out of the vale of Hebron; the witnesses note Jacob charging Joseph to see whether it were well with his brethren and the flocks and bring word, sending him from Hebron to Shechem — the loving errand of a father's care, sending Joseph the long way to his brethren ('Go... see whether it be well with thy brethren, and well with the flocks; and bring me word again. So he sent him out of the vale of Hebron, and he came to Shechem'; the tenderness of the charge, that Jacob, in fatherly care, would know 'whether it be well with' his sons 'and... the flocks,' and bids Joseph 'bring me word again,' sending him to inquire after their welfare; the length and peril of the journey, from 'the vale of Hebron' in the south to 'Shechem' far to the north, a long and lonely way for a youth of seventeen; that Joseph, in obedience, 'came to Shechem,' seeking his brethren; the irony that the errand of love would be the occasion of the brothers' cruelty, so Joseph, sent in his father's love, comes to Shechem).

themes: Go, I pray thee, see whether it be well with thy brethren... and bring me word again
So he sent him out of the vale of Hebron, and he came to Shechem
Jacob's loving charge, sending Joseph the long way to his brethren
the errand of a father's care made the occasion of the brothers' cruelty

schools.summary: Gill: many of the Jewish writers say this man was an angel, even Gabriel in the likeness of a man, but Aben Ezra thinks it was a traveller he met on the way; the witnesses note a certain man finding Joseph wandering in the field and asking what he sought — the providential meeting that directed Joseph onward, the unnamed man who set him on his way (Joseph, not finding his brethren at Shechem, wandering until a man meets him: 'a certain man found him, and, behold, he was wandering in the field: and the man asked him, saying, What seekest thou?'; Joseph's plight, that he had come to Shechem but found his brethren gone and was 'wandering in the field,' at a loss where to seek them; the meeting with 'a certain man' who, finding him so, asks 'What seekest thou?'; the speculation of the commentators, that some of the Jewish writers took this man to be an angel, Gabriel, sent to direct Joseph, while others, Aben Ezra, take him for an ordinary traveller; that, angel or man, the meeting was of God's providence, the seemingly chance encounter that kept Joseph from turning back and set him on the road to find his brethren and his appointed trial, so the man finds the wandering Joseph and asks his errand).

debates.question: Who was the 'certain man' who found Joseph wandering?

sides.position: An ordinary traveller Joseph met on the way (Aben Ezra and many)
An angel (Gabriel) in human likeness, sent to direct Joseph (some Jewish writers)

themes: a certain man found him, and, behold, he was wandering in the field
the man asked him, saying, What seekest thou?
the providential meeting that directed Joseph onward
angel or man, the encounter that set him on the road to his trial

schools.summary: Gill: Joseph described his brethren to the man, who they were and to whom they belonged, or otherwise the man would have been at a loss to know who he meant; the witnesses note Joseph answering the man that he sought his brethren and asking where they fed their flocks — the simple, earnest reply of the seeking brother, intent on finding his own (Joseph telling his errand: 'I seek my brethren: tell me, I pray thee, where they feed their flocks'; the plainness of the answer, that Joseph, asked what he sought, replies simply 'I seek my brethren' and begs to be told 'where they feed their flocks' that he might find them; the touching note the commentators draw, that Joseph 'sought his brethren,' the very ones who hated him, with a brother's heart, intent only on doing his father's errand and seeing them well; the typology, that here Joseph, like Christ, 'sought his brethren' though they hated him, a fit emblem of the Saviour who came seeking his own; that this earnest seeking led him on to the place of his betrayal, so Joseph, seeking his brethren, asks the way to them).

themes: I seek my brethren
tell me, I pray thee, where they feed their flocks
Joseph seeking his brethren with a brother's heart
a fit emblem of Christ, who sought his own though they hated him

schools.summary: Gill: they had been in the field where Joseph and the man were, but for good reasons had departed to Dothan; the witnesses note the man telling Joseph his brethren had gone to Dothan, and Joseph going after them and finding them there — the providential word that sent Joseph on to Dothan, where his brethren were (the man's word guiding Joseph onward: 'They are departed hence; for I heard them say, Let us go to Dothan. And Joseph went after his brethren, and found them in Dothan'; the providence in the man's overhearing, that he had 'heard them say, Let us go to Dothan,' and so could tell Joseph where to seek them; Joseph's perseverance, that he did not give up when he missed them at Shechem but 'went after his brethren' the further way to 'Dothan,' some miles north, and 'found them' there; that this further journey, undertaken in obedience and brotherly love, brought Joseph at last to the very place where the brothers would seize him; the working of God's hand through the chance word of a stranger, ordering all toward the fulfilment of the dreams, so Joseph, guided to Dothan, finds his brethren).

themes: They are departed hence; for I heard them say, Let us go to Dothan
And Joseph went after his brethren, and found them in Dothan
the providence in a stranger's overheard word
Joseph persevering in obedience, brought at last to the place of his seizing

schools.summary: Gill: they knew him as soon as they saw him, by his stature, his gesture or manner of walking, and especially by his coat of various colours he now had on; the witnesses note the brethren, seeing Joseph afar off, conspiring against him to slay him before he came near — the cold and deliberate plotting of murder, hatred ripened into a conspiracy to kill (the sight of Joseph kindling the brothers' hatred into deadly purpose: 'when they saw him afar off, even before he came near unto them, they conspired against him to slay him'; how they knew him, that they recognised Joseph 'afar off' by his stature, his manner of walking, and especially by 'his coat of many colours,' the badge of their father's favour and the very thing they hated; the dreadful deliberateness of the deed, that, 'before he came near,' while he was yet far off, they 'conspired against him to slay him,' taking counsel together coldly to murder their own brother; the depth of the hatred, that they would not even wait or hear him but plotted his death at the first sight; the typology, that Joseph, like Christ, was conspired against by his own brethren, so the brothers, seeing Joseph, conspire to kill him).

themes: when they saw him afar off, even before he came near unto them
they conspired against him to slay him
the cold, deliberate plotting of murder against their own brother
Joseph, like Christ, conspired against by his own

schools.summary: Gill: the Targum of Jonathan says Simeon and Levi said this, nor is it unlikely since they were hot, passionate, cruel, and bloody-minded men; the witnesses note the brethren saying one to another, 'Behold, this dreamer cometh' — the scornful naming of Joseph by his dreams, mocking the very thing they feared (the brothers greeting Joseph's coming with a sneer: 'Behold, this dreamer cometh'; the contempt in the word, that they call him not by his name but 'this dreamer,' literally 'this master of dreams' or 'lord of dreams,' a mocking title that flung back at him the dreams of his coming rule; that their scorn betrayed their fear, for they hated the dreams because they feared they were true, that Joseph should indeed reign over them; the bitter irony the commentators mark, that in mocking 'this dreamer' they were about to set in motion the very events that would make the dreams come true, their cruelty being the first step toward Joseph's exaltation; the report that Simeon and Levi, the hot and bloody brothers, led the scorn, so the brothers mock Joseph as the dreamer).

themes: And they said one to another, Behold, this dreamer cometh
the scornful naming of Joseph by his dreams
their scorn betraying their fear that the dreams were true
the bitter irony of setting in motion the dreams' fulfilment

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: what unprincipled savages these must have been to talk thus coolly of murdering their brother; Gill: they agreed to slay him and cast him into a pit, and to cover it with a lie; the witnesses note the brethren plotting to slay Joseph, cast him into a pit, and say a beast devoured him, to see what would become of his dreams — the cold scheme of murder and concealment, with the proud taunt against God's foreshowing (the brothers laying the whole plot: 'let us slay him, and cast him into some pit, and we will say, Some evil beast hath devoured him: and we shall see what will become of his dreams'; the three parts of the wickedness, the murder, 'let us slay him,' the concealment, 'cast him into some pit,' and the lie to deceive their father, 'we will say, Some evil beast hath devoured him'; the coldness Clarke marks, that they could plot such a thing of their own brother so calmly; the proud and impious taunt, 'we shall see what will become of his dreams,' as if by killing Joseph they could defeat God's purpose and prove the dreams false; the great irony, that this very deed would be the means of fulfilling the dreams, the pit and the selling being the first steps to Egypt and the throne, so the brothers plot Joseph's death and mock his dreams).

themes: let us slay him, and cast him into some pit
and we shall see what will become of his dreams
the cold plot of murder, concealment, and a lie
the impious taunt against the dreams, the deed that would fulfil them

schools.summary: Gill: Reuben overheard what they said, not being in the consultation; being of a milder disposition and the elder brother, he sought to deliver him; the witnesses note Reuben hearing the plot and delivering Joseph out of their hands, saying, 'Let us not kill him' — the firstborn's intervention to save Joseph's life, turning the murder aside (one brother's conscience stirring against the deed: 'Reuben heard it, and he delivered him out of their hands; and said, Let us not kill him'; Reuben's part, that he 'heard' the murderous plot and, being of a gentler nature and the eldest, would not consent to it but 'delivered him out of their hands,' saved him from immediate death, and pleaded, 'Let us not kill him'; the commentators' reading of Reuben's motive, that, partly from natural tenderness and partly, as the next verse shows, hoping to rescue Joseph secretly and restore him to his father, perhaps to regain his own lost favour after his sin, Reuben sought to turn the brothers from blood; that, whatever his mixed motive, Reuben's word stayed the murder, God using even his imperfect intervention to preserve Joseph alive).

themes: And Reuben heard it, and he delivered him out of their hands
and said, Let us not kill him
the firstborn's intervention to save Joseph's life
God using even Reuben's imperfect intervention to preserve Joseph

schools.summary: Gill: Reuben said shed no blood, the blood of a man, a brother's blood, one that had not done anything wherefore it should be shed; his purpose was to rid him out of their hands and deliver him to his father; the witnesses note Reuben counselling them to shed no blood but cast Joseph into a pit, intending to rescue him and restore him to his father — the prudent stratagem by which Reuben sought to save Joseph alive (Reuben turning the brothers from murder to a lesser thing, with a secret purpose: 'Shed no blood, but cast him into this pit... and lay no hand upon him; that he might rid him out of their hands, to deliver him to his father again'; the wisdom of Reuben's plea, that, knowing he could not at once persuade them to let Joseph go free, he urges them to 'shed no blood' and 'lay no hand upon him' but only 'cast him into this pit,' a lesser evil that left Joseph alive; the narrator's disclosure of Reuben's true intent, 'that he might rid him out of their hands, to deliver him to his father again,' that Reuben meant to come back secretly and draw Joseph out of the pit and restore him to Jacob; that Reuben's plan, good in aim though weak in courage, for he did not openly defy the rest, was overruled by God, who had another way to save Joseph).

themes: Shed no blood, but cast him into this pit... and lay no hand upon him
that he might rid him out of their hands, to deliver him to his father again
Reuben's stratagem to save Joseph by the lesser evil of the pit
his secret purpose to rescue him, overruled by God's other way

schools.summary: Gill: that when Joseph was come to his brethren, having in a kind and obliging manner asked of their welfare and related their father's concern, they stript him of his coat; the witnesses note the brethren, when Joseph came to them, stripping him of his coat of many colours — the violent seizing and stripping of Joseph, the badge of his father's love torn from him (the brothers falling upon Joseph at his coming: 'when Joseph was come unto his brethren, that they stript Joseph out of his coat, his coat of many colours that was on him'; the cruelty and meaning of the act, that Joseph, having come in love and asked after their welfare, was met not with greeting but with violence, 'stript... out of his coat,' the very 'coat of many colours' that marked their father's favour; that in tearing off the coat they tore off the token of the love they envied, venting their hatred on the garment as on the brother; the typology, that Christ too was stripped of his garments by his enemies; that the coat, so stripped, would soon serve their lie, so the brothers strip Joseph of his coat).

themes: when Joseph was come unto his brethren
they stript Joseph out of his coat, his coat of many colours that was on him
the brothers venting their hatred on the badge of their father's love
Joseph met not with greeting but violence, as Christ too was stripped

schools.summary: Gill: they cast him into the same pit Reuben had pointed to, whose counsel they gladly took and readily executed, supposing he meant the same thing they did; the pit was empty, with no water in it; the witnesses note the brethren taking Joseph and casting him into a pit that was empty, with no water in it — the casting of Joseph into the dry pit, a living grave, his cries unheeded (the brothers doing the deed: 'they took him, and cast him into a pit: and the pit was empty, there was no water in it'; the act, that they 'took him, and cast him into a pit,' the cistern Reuben had named, treating their brother as a thing to be thrown away; the detail, 'the pit was empty, there was no water in it,' a dry cistern so that Joseph did not drown but was left in the deep, dark pit, alive and helpless; from the later account, that Joseph cried to them from the pit 'in the anguish of his soul,' begging for mercy, and they would not hear, a hardness they would remember with grief long after; the typology, that Joseph in the pit, as one in the grave, foreshadows Christ, so Joseph is cast into the empty pit, alive but forsaken).

themes: And they took him, and cast him into a pit
and the pit was empty, there was no water in it
Joseph cast into the dry pit, a living grave, alive but forsaken
his cries for mercy unheeded, a hardness they would later grieve

schools.summary: Gill: they sat down to eat bread, not at all concerned at what they had done, nor in the least grieved for the affliction of Joseph; the witnesses note the brethren sitting to eat bread while Joseph lay in the pit, and an Ishmaelite caravan from Gilead appearing, going to Egypt — the callous feasting beside the pit, and the providential coming of the merchants (the brothers, their brother in the pit, sitting down to a meal: 'they sat down to eat bread: and they lifted up their eyes and looked, and, behold, a company of Ishmeelites came from Gilead... going to carry it down to Egypt'; the hardness of heart Gill marks, that they could 'sit down to eat bread' with Joseph crying in the pit nearby, 'not at all concerned' nor grieved at his affliction, a chilling picture of conscience seared by hatred; the providence in what they then saw, 'a company of Ishmeelites,' the descendants of Ishmael, merchants, 'came from Gilead with their camels bearing spicery and balm and myrrh, going to carry it down to Egypt'; that this caravan, passing just then, was no chance but God's ordering, the very means by which Joseph would be brought to Egypt, the place of his appointed rise, so the brothers feast while the merchants providentially appear).

themes: they sat down to eat bread
a company of Ishmeelites came from Gilead... going to carry it down to Egypt
the callous feast beside the pit, unconcerned at Joseph's affliction
the providential caravan, God's ordering of the means to bring Joseph to Egypt

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: Judah said unto his brethren, What profit is it if we slay our brother and conceal his blood?; Gill notes Judah, in sight of the Ishmaelites, had a thought to get Joseph sold to them; the witnesses note Judah asking what profit there was in slaying Joseph and concealing his blood — the turn from murder to sale, Judah's mingled motive of gain and a flicker of conscience (the caravan in sight, Judah proposing another course: 'What profit is it if we slay our brother, and conceal his blood?'; the two strands in Judah's word, on the one hand a flicker of natural feeling, that he would not have them 'slay our brother' and have his blood on their hands, but on the other the low motive of 'profit,' that there was no gain in killing him whereas they might make money by selling him; that Judah's was no pure repentance but a mingling of some conscience against murder with covetousness, seeking a way that was less bloody yet still wicked; that, though base, his proposal was used of God to spare Joseph's life and send him to Egypt; the owning of the tie, 'our brother,' even in their cruelty the bond confessed, so Judah proposes selling rather than slaying Joseph).

themes: And Judah said unto his brethren, What profit is it if we slay our brother
and conceal his blood?
Judah's turn from murder to sale, conscience mingled with covetousness
a base motive yet used of God to spare Joseph's life

schools.summary: Gill: the thought was to sell him for a slave, and that would defeat his dream, and as they were going to Egypt he would be far enough off; the witnesses note Judah counselling them to sell Joseph to the Ishmaelites and not lay hands on him, since he was their brother, and the brethren being content — the agreement to sell Joseph into slavery, a cruelty cloaked as mercy (Judah completing his proposal and the brothers consenting: 'let us sell him to the Ishmeelites, and let not our hand be upon him; for he is our brother and our flesh. And his brethren were content'; the show of mercy and the real cruelty, that Judah pleads 'let not our hand be upon him; for he is our brother and our flesh,' as if sparing him, yet the counsel was to 'sell him' into bondage among strangers, a living death; the hidden purpose Gill marks, that by selling Joseph far off to Egypt they thought to 'defeat his dream,' putting him where he could never rule over them; the great irony, that the very selling into Egypt was the road to the throne, the means of fulfilling the dream they sought to defeat; that 'his brethren were content,' agreeing to the base bargain; the typology, that Christ too was sold for money, so the brothers agree to sell Joseph).

themes: let us sell him to the Ishmeelites... for he is our brother and our flesh
And his brethren were content
the agreement to sell Joseph, cruelty cloaked as mercy
thinking to defeat his dream, yet the very road to the throne

schools.summary: Gill: the Midianites are the same with the Ishmaelites, near neighbours; Joseph was sold for twenty pieces of silver and brought down to Egypt; the witnesses note the brethren drawing Joseph out of the pit and selling him to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver, who brought him into Egypt — the selling of the betrayed brother for silver and his carrying down to Egypt (the deed done: 'they drew and lifted up Joseph out of the pit, and sold Joseph to the Ishmeelites for twenty pieces of silver: and they brought Joseph into Egypt'; the act, that they 'drew... up Joseph out of the pit' only to sell him, handing their brother over 'for twenty pieces of silver,' the price of a young slave; the question of the 'Midianites' and 'Ishmaelites,' the commentators taking them as the same caravan or near-kindred merchants travelling together, to whom Joseph was sold; the price, 'twenty pieces of silver,' and the typology the commentators draw, that as Joseph was sold for silver by his brethren, so Christ would be sold for thirty pieces by Judas; the great providence, 'they brought Joseph into Egypt,' the betrayed brother carried down to the land where he would be exalted, God's hand in the very wrong, so Joseph is sold for silver and brought to Egypt).

debates.question: What is the typological significance of Joseph's sale for silver?

sides.position: As Joseph was sold for twenty pieces of silver by his brethren, so Christ would be sold for thirty pieces of silver by Judas — the betrayed and sold brother a type of the betrayed Saviour

themes: they drew and lifted up Joseph out of the pit, and sold Joseph... for twenty pieces of silver
and they brought Joseph into Egypt
the betrayed brother sold for silver, as Christ by Judas
God's hand in the wrong, bringing Joseph to the land of his exaltation

schools.summary: Gill: it is probable Reuben had pretended to go somewhere on business, with an intention to take a circuit and come to the pit and deliver his brother; the witnesses note Reuben returning to the pit, finding Joseph gone, and rending his clothes — the elder brother's grief and dismay at finding his secret rescue too late (Reuben coming back to keep his hidden purpose, and being undone: 'Reuben returned unto the pit; and, behold, Joseph was not in the pit; and he rent his clothes'; Reuben's design and its failure, that he had withdrawn, probably on some pretext, meaning to come back secretly and 'deliver his brother' out of the pit and restore him to Jacob, but he 'returned unto the pit' to find it empty, 'Joseph was not in the pit,' the selling having been done in his absence; his grief, 'he rent his clothes,' the token of deep distress and mourning, that his plan was thwarted and his brother gone he knew not where; the weakness mixed with his good intent, that had he stood up openly against the rest he might have saved Joseph, but his secret way failed, so Reuben, finding the pit empty, rends his clothes in dismay).

themes: And Reuben returned unto the pit; and, behold, Joseph was not in the pit
and he rent his clothes
Reuben's grief at finding his secret rescue too late
his good intent undone by a hidden way rather than an open stand

schools.summary: Gill: he returned from the pit to his brethren, whom he suspected had taken and killed Joseph, not being with them when they proposed to sell him; the witnesses note Reuben returning to his brethren and crying, 'The child is not; and I, whither shall I go?' — the eldest brother's anguish, knowing himself answerable to his father for the lost boy (Reuben pouring out his distress: 'The child is not; and I, whither shall I go?'; the grief in his words, 'The child is not,' Joseph the lad gone as if dead or lost beyond recovery; the special weight on Reuben, 'I, whither shall I go?' that, as the eldest, he felt himself most answerable to his father for Joseph's safety, and now, the boy gone, he knew not how to face Jacob or where to turn; that Reuben did not yet know what had become of Joseph, fearing the worst that the others had killed him, being absent when they sold him; the burden of responsibility on the firstborn, and his helpless dismay, so Reuben cries out his anguish to his brethren).

themes: he returned unto his brethren, and said, The child is not
and I, whither shall I go?
Reuben's anguish over the lost boy
the eldest feeling himself most answerable to his father, in helpless dismay

schools.summary: Gill: after telling Reuben what they had done, they joined in the following scheme, taking the coat and dipping it in the blood of a kid; the witnesses note the brethren taking Joseph's coat, killing a kid, and dipping the coat in the blood — the contriving of the cruel deception, the bloody coat to deceive their father (the brothers fashioning their lie: 'they took Joseph's coat, and killed a kid of the goats, and dipped the coat in the blood'; the calculated deceit, that they took 'Joseph's coat,' the coat of many colours they had stripped from him, 'killed a kid of the goats,' and 'dipped the coat in the blood,' to make it seem that a wild beast had slain Joseph and stained his coat with his blood; the bitter justice the commentators mark, that Jacob, who had once deceived his own father with a kid of the goats and Esau's garment, is now himself deceived by his sons with a kid's blood and Joseph's coat, the deceiver deceived; the cold deliberateness of the plot to break their father's heart with a lie, so the brothers prepare the bloody coat to deceive Jacob).

debates.question: What bitter justice do the commentators mark in the bloody coat?

sides.position: Jacob, who once deceived his blind father Isaac with a kid of the goats and Esau's garment (Genesis 27), is now himself deceived by his sons with a kid's blood and Joseph's coat — the deceiver deceived

themes: they took Joseph's coat, and killed a kid of the goats
and dipped the coat in the blood
the contriving of the bloody coat to deceive their father
Jacob the deceiver now deceived by a kid and a garment, as he once deceived

schools.summary: Gill: they sent the coat, dipped in the blood of the kid, to Jacob in such a condition by the hand of some messenger; the witnesses note the brethren sending the coat of many colours to their father, saying, 'This have we found: know now whether it be thy son's coat or no' — the heartless deception laid before the father, the coat sent with a cold, feigned question (the brothers carrying out the lie: 'they sent the coat... and said, This have we found: know now whether it be thy son's coat or no'; the cruelty and cunning of it, that they did not say plainly 'Joseph is dead' but sent the bloodied coat with the cold words 'This have we found,' feigning ignorance, and asked Jacob himself to 'know' whether it were his son's coat, leaving him to draw the dreadful conclusion; the hard-heartedness, that they could thus coldly deceive their own father and watch him break under the false news; the studied distance in their words, not naming Joseph nor owning any part but letting the coat and the blood tell the lie, so the brothers send the bloody coat with a feigned question).

themes: they sent the coat of many colours, and they brought it to their father
This have we found: know now whether it be thy son's coat or no
the bloody coat sent with a cold, feigned question
not naming Joseph, leaving Jacob to draw the dreadful conclusion

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob examined it and was soon convinced and well assured it was his son's coat; the witnesses note Jacob knowing the coat and crying that an evil beast had devoured Joseph, who was rent in pieces — the father's fatal conclusion, drawn exactly as the brothers intended (the lie doing its work: 'he knew it, and said, It is my son's coat; an evil beast hath devoured him; Joseph is without doubt rent in pieces'; that Jacob, examining the bloodied coat, 'knew it' at once for 'my son's coat,' and drew the very conclusion the brothers had planned, 'an evil beast hath devoured him; Joseph is without doubt rent in pieces'; the irony and grief, that the deception succeeded so well that Jacob never suspected his sons but believed Joseph torn by a wild beast and dead; the providence even here, that God let Jacob be deceived for a time, the truth hidden through long years until the day of revealing in Egypt; that the father's certainty, 'without doubt,' shows how complete the deception was, so Jacob, deceived, concludes Joseph is devoured).

themes: he knew it, and said, It is my son's coat; an evil beast hath devoured him
Joseph is without doubt rent in pieces
the father's fatal conclusion, drawn exactly as planned
Jacob never suspecting his sons, the truth hidden until Egypt

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Jacob mourned for Joseph not merely during the usual days of mourning but many days, refusing to be comforted; Gill: Jacob rent his clothes as expressive of his grief for the death of his son, as he supposed; the witnesses note Jacob rending his clothes, putting on sackcloth, and mourning for his son many days — the father's overwhelming, prolonged grief for the son he believed dead (Jacob giving himself wholly to mourning: 'Jacob rent his clothes, and put sackcloth upon his loins, and mourned for his son many days'; the tokens of deep grief, that Jacob 'rent his clothes' and 'put sackcloth upon his loins,' the customary signs of the bitterest mourning, and 'mourned for his son many days,' not the usual term only but long beyond it, his grief refusing to be assuaged; the depth of a father's love in this, that Jacob, who loved Joseph above all, was broken by his supposed death and would not be comforted; the irony and sorrow, that Jacob mourned as dead one who was yet alive, his grief unnecessary yet unrelieved, the fruit of his sons' cruelty; that God, who saw it all, let the old man's grief run on, the dark night before the distant dawn, so Jacob mourns deeply for Joseph many days).

themes: Jacob rent his clothes, and put sackcloth upon his loins
and mourned for his son many days
Jacob's overwhelming grief, beyond the usual term
broken by the supposed death of the son he loved above all

schools.summary: Gill: his sons must act a most hypocritical part in this affair, rising up to comfort him over a grief they themselves had caused (and his daughters, it is not easy to say who they were); the witnesses note all Jacob's sons and daughters rising to comfort him, but he refusing comfort, resolving to go down to the grave mourning — the inconsolable grief of the father, and the hypocrisy of the sons who caused it (the family gathering to comfort, in vain: 'all his sons and all his daughters rose up to comfort him; but he refused to be comforted... For I will go down into the grave unto my son mourning'; the hypocrisy Gill marks, that 'all his sons' who 'rose up to comfort him' included the very brothers who had sold Joseph and forged the lie, acting a part, feigning sympathy for a sorrow they had cruelly caused, a deep and shameless deceit; the depth of Jacob's grief, that 'he refused to be comforted,' would take no consolation, and vowed, 'I will go down into the grave unto my son mourning,' resolved to mourn Joseph all his days, to death; the tenderness in 'his father wept for him,' the old man's tears; that God let this sorrow stand, the truth still hidden, the long trial of Jacob's faith begun).

themes: he refused to be comforted; and he said, For I will go down into the grave unto my son mourning
Thus his father wept for him
Jacob's inconsolable grief, resolved to mourn to the grave
the hypocrisy of the sons who feigned comfort for a grief they caused

schools.summary: Gill: the Midianites (or Medanites, who sprung from Medan, a brother of Midian) sold him into Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh's, captain of the guard; the witnesses note the Midianites selling Joseph in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh and captain of the guard — the placing of Joseph in Potiphar's house, God's providence setting him where his rise would begin (the chapter closing by following Joseph to Egypt: 'the Midianites sold him into Egypt unto Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh's, and captain of the guard'; that the merchants, having brought Joseph down, 'sold him... unto Potiphar,' a man of rank, 'an officer of Pharaoh's, and captain of the guard,' the chief of the royal guard or keeper of the prison; the providence the commentators mark, that Joseph was not sold to any common master but into the house of a great officer near the throne, the very place from which, through trial, he would rise to power; that the chapter ends in seeming defeat, Joseph a slave in a strange land his dreams apparently buried, yet this was the first step of God's hidden plan to exalt him and save many alive; the comfort, that God's providence was guiding all, even the deepest wrong, toward good, so Joseph is sold to Potiphar in Egypt, his rise begun).

themes: And the Midianites sold him into Egypt unto Potiphar
an officer of Pharaoh's, and captain of the guard
Joseph placed in Potiphar's house, near the throne
God's providence setting him where his rise would begin, even in seeming defeat
